title: cor:psd: classical-vs-distributional Hessian bridge is hand-waved; PSD claimed at all z including degenerate spectra

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
In Corollary `cor:psd` the identity $\operatorname{Hess}_{(a,b)}L=\sum_i c_i\operatorname{Hess}_{(a,b)}\lambda_i$ is written CLASSICALLY, yet the individual $\lambda_i$ are not convex (only the partial sums $\sigma_k$ are). PSD-ness is then obtained from '$\operatorname{Hess}\sigma_k\succeq0$ wherever twice differentiable' plus, at degeneracies, '$\operatorname{Hess}\sigma_k\succeq0$ in the distributional sense.' The bridge between (i) Abel-summing convex $\sigma_k$, (ii) classical Hessians of the non-convex $\lambda_i$, and (iii) the distributional statement, is not made rigorous. Moreover the boxed conclusion asserts $\sum_j d_jH^{(j)}\succeq0$ for ALL $z$ and all $d_1\ge\dots\ge d_n$, including points where hyperbolic eigenvalues collide and $\sigma_k$ is not $C^2$.

Suggested fix: restrict to the open set $U=\{z:\lambda_1(z)>\dots>\lambda_n(z)\}$ where each $\sigma_k$ is real-analytic (implicit function theorem on the relevant factor of $f(z-te)$); there 'convex $+\,C^2\Rightarrow$ PSD Hessian' is standard and Abel summation gives $\operatorname{Hess}L\succeq0$ classically. Then note that after the reduction to simple roots the evaluation point (where $\gamma_j$ are distinct, equivalently the $\lambda_i$ distinct) lies in $U$, which is all the application needs — eliminating the distributional language entirely.
